description: Evaluates personal-writer skill output for structural consistency, formatting rules, and required elements across 5 distinct test prompts. 25 total assertions.

test_cases.prompt: Write a blog post about why I switched from Twitter to Bluesky after 10 years
test_cases.context: Core use case: personal experience narrative blog post

assertions.check: Total output is under 600 words
Total output is at least 250 words (not a stub)
Does not contain em-dashes (—)
Does not contain 'In conclusion'
Output contains a personal anecdote or specific moment — not just generic observations about the platforms

test_cases.prompt: Write a short post about what I learned from failing my first startup
test_cases.context: Personal reflection with lessons learned — tests structure and vulnerable tone

assertions.check: Does not contain 'In summary'
Output includes at least one concrete, actionable takeaway the reader can apply — not just reflection
Does not use the word 'utilize'
Under 500 words
Final paragraph ends with something forward-looking or a call-to-action — not a wistful reflection or open question

test_cases.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about why I think most product roadmaps are wrong
test_cases.context: Opinionated professional post — tests takes, brevity, and no corporate-speak

assertions.check: Under 300 words (LinkedIn sweet spot)
Does not use the word 'leverage' as a verb
Does not contain 'synergy' or 'synergies'
First sentence makes a strong, specific claim — not a question, not 'I want to talk about', not throat-clearing
Post contains at least one specific number or concrete example (not just abstract argument)

test_cases.prompt: Write a newsletter intro for this week's edition about my experience moving from Figma to linear workflows
test_cases.context: Newsletter format — tests structure markers, personal voice, and appropriate length

assertions.check: Under 200 words (intro, not full piece)
At least 80 words (not too sparse)
Does not start with 'Hey' or 'Hello' as the first word of the intro body
Output has a clear 3-part structure: hook/opening, the story/context, and a bridge to the rest of the newsletter
Ends with a sentence that creates forward momentum — makes the reader want to keep reading

test_cases.prompt: Write a post about the one productivity habit that actually changed how I work
test_cases.context: Productivity content — tests specificity, avoiding clichés, and having a real point

assertions.check: Does not mention 'morning routine' as the habit
Does not use 'game-changer' or 'game changer'
The habit is described specifically enough that a reader could actually try it — not just 'focus more' or 'be intentional'
Under 400 words
Post reads in first person and maintains a consistent 'I' voice throughout — not shifting to 'you' or 'we' for the main narrative

_notes: 5 test prompts × 5 assertions = 25 total assertions. Covers: blog post, personal reflection, LinkedIn, newsletter, productivity content. Assertions focus on structural rules (word count), forbidden patterns (em-dashes, corporate speak, clichés), and required elements (concrete examples, actionable takeaways, strong openings).
